filters.label: End-to-end ownership
filters.description: Claims ownership of a build from problem to working system.

filters.label: Shipped to production
filters.description: States a system reached real users / production.

filters.label: Concrete hard decision
filters.description: Names a specific difficult decision and what they decided.

filters.label: Governance / risk thinking
filters.description: Raises risk, fairness, security, or governance unprompted.

filters.label: Used AI tools
filters.description: Describes using AI tools and what judgment they kept.

filters.label: Non-traditional builder
filters.description: Built via low-code / config / orchestration or a self-taught path.

filters.label: Cross-artifact conflict
filters.description: Resume and answers appear to disagree — reviewer to interpret.

static_questions.text: Describe something you have built end to end, from problem to working system. What was the hardest decision you faced, and what did you decide?
Describe a time you used AI tools in your work. What did you hand off to the AI, and what judgment did you keep for yourself?
Describe a project where you balanced competing priorities, such as speed against risk or governance. How did you decide what to do?

blocks.text: Architected and owned an enterprise AI platform end to end, transforming how the organization operates.
I led the entire build from problem to system. The hardest part was managing complexity, and I drove it to success.

evidence.quote: owned an enterprise AI platform end to end
I led the entire build from problem to system

blocks.text: Backend engineer. Built an internal data pipeline.

I built a deduplication service that processes 12M records nightly; the hard call was accepting eventual consistency to hit the latency budget, which I decided was acceptable given downstream tolerance.

evidence.quote: I built a deduplication service
processes 12M records nightly
the hard call was accepting eventual consistency to hit the latency budget

blocks.text: Collaborated with cross-functional teams to drive impactful AI initiatives and contributed to several high-visibility programs.
I was involved in building a recommendation feature and contributed to many discussions about its direction.
I used AI tools to help draft content, and I reviewed everything the team produced.

evidence.quote: I used AI tools to help draft content

blocks.text: Shipped a route-optimization tool used daily by 40 drivers.
I built a scheduling system; the hardest decision was dropping real-time traffic data because the API was unreliable, so I used historical averages instead.

evidence.quote: I built a scheduling system
used daily by 40 drivers
the hardest decision was dropping real-time traffic data because the API was unreliable

blocks.text: Led a team of five engineers to deliver a fraud-detection model.
On the fraud project I supported the lead and implemented the feature store.

evidence.quote: Led a team of five engineers
I supported the lead

blocks.text: Built a customer-support assistant deployed to production.
I balanced speed against risk by gating the model behind a human review queue for low-confidence outputs, and I logged every decision for auditability.

evidence.quote: deployed to production
gating the model behind a human review queue
I logged every decision for auditability

blocks.text: Built and shipped a real-time analytics dashboard to production.
I designed the streaming architecture; the hardest decision was choosing Kafka over a managed queue to control cost at scale.

evidence.quote: shipped a real-time analytics dashboard to production
choosing Kafka over a managed queue to control cost at scale

blocks.text: Operations lead. Automated invoice processing using Zapier and Airtable, no code.
I built an order-routing workflow with low-code tools and a small script, cutting manual handling by 70%.

evidence.quote: using Zapier and Airtable, no code
with low-code tools
I built an order-routing workflow
cutting manual handling by 70%
